channel.title: Agent Nouveau — Facebook
channel.description: Agent Nouveau journal — Facebook editorial feed

item.title: ⟡ A larder a few steps wide
item.description:
⟡ A larder a few steps wide
What lifts Oro out of the ordinary run of hotel dining rooms is where the food comes from. Much of it grows on the island. The Cipriani keeps its own organic gardens on Giudecca, sending salad leaves, herbs and edible flowers up to the kitchens through the day, picked rather than ordered.
The rest comes from the lagoon at the door, the moeche and the seasonal fish the city has eaten for centuries. The cooking does not have to travel far to be honest. It mostly just has to look down. 🖤
A table here is worth planning ahead for.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ A room called gold
item.description:
⟡ A room called gold
Oro means gold, and the room earns it. The dining room sits beneath a domed ceiling worked in gold leaf, lit by a handmade Murano-glass chandelier, the whole space turned by the designer Adam Tihany into something that glows rather than glitters.
It would be easy for a room like this to overplay its hand. It does not. The gold is the ceiling's business. At eye level the setting is calm, the linen plain, the windows giving onto the water and the lights of the city beyond.
Read the full story of Oro on our journal.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Hour Before the Heat
item.description:
⟡ The Hour Before the Heat
A safari wakes you at half past five, in the dark, with coffee you drink before you can see the cup. By half past six you are on the vehicle, wrapped in a blanket against a cold most people do not associate with Africa, watching the bush come back to itself as the light arrives.
It is the earliest most of our clients have risen in years, and almost without exception it becomes the part they miss most. Not the leopard. The quiet. The cold air. The hour before the heat, when nothing is asked of you and the whole day is still ahead.
Our new journal piece on the bush as a reset, and why the dawn is the point.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ A clock you do not keep yourself
item.description:
⟡ A clock you do not keep yourself
Most of a game drive is not pursuit. It is stillness. The vehicle stops, the engine goes off, and you sit. A guide reads the ground, points out a bird you would never have noticed, and lets the silence sit. There is no signal for your phone, and after the first morning you stop reaching for it.
That is the first kindness of the bush. The clock is being kept for you... which means, for the first time in a long while, you are not keeping it yourself. The falling-in is the whole therapy.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The middle of the day is empty by design
item.description:
⟡ The middle of the day is empty by design
Between the two drives there is a stretch of hours with nothing in them. At home that would feel like waste. In the bush it feels like the point. The heat climbs, the animals lie up in the shade, and the camp goes soft and slow.
This enforced pause is the thing modern travel has quietly engineered out. We fill the gaps. We optimise the day. We come home from a holiday needing a holiday. The bush does not offer that option, and a few days of it resets something a city break leaves untouched.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Wine Became the Reason
item.description:
⟡ The Wine Became the Reason
Global wine tourism is now a sector worth around fifty-one billion dollars a year. The interesting part is not the number. It is what the number describes.
For a long time, wine was a stop on a holiday. A morning out from the city, a tasting on a schedule, a clock running over the whole thing. That has quietly changed. Wine tourism now accounts for roughly a quarter of global winery revenue, which means the estates that pay attention are building the visit itself, not just selling the bottle. The wine, the valley and the table have become the reason for the journey.
Our new Travel Intelligence piece on wine travel growing up.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The day trip and the stay
item.description:
⟡ The day trip and the stay
There are two ways to travel for wine, and the difference is the whole thing.
The day trip extracts. A morning out from the city, a tasting booked to the minute, a designated driver who cannot taste a thing, and a clock running over the table. The stay restores. It gives the valley enough hours to register, lets a lunch run its natural length, and asks nothing of the afternoon afterwards.
This year Virtuoso, who plan for travellers much like ours, reported that dining has moved from a perk to a pillar of the trip. People are no longer fitting wine into a holiday. They are building one around it... and we think they are right to.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Where this lands
item.description:
⟡ Where this lands
In South Africa, wine tourism contributed 9.3 billion rand to the economy and made up around seventeen per cent of the wine industry's turnover. Six Cape vineyards now sit among the world's fifty best.
The valleys an hour east of Cape Town, around Stellenbosch and Franschhoek, are built for exactly the kind of travel the figures keep pointing towards. They run on long lunches and late light. They reward staying in the valley over driving out for the day. And they fall outside the European summer, which is its own quiet argument for going. That is the thread we are following through June.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Grape That Belongs Nowhere Else
item.description:
⟡ The Grape That Belongs Nowhere Else
The first wine at the Cape was pressed on the second of February, 1659. We know the date because the man responsible wrote it down in his diary, in the plain relief of someone who had been waiting. South Africa is one of the few wine countries that can name the morning it began.
It is usually filed under New World wine and sold on price, which misses almost everything. By 1685 the sweet wine of Constantia was travelling to the courts of Europe... the first New World wine to win real international acclaim, a hundred years before most of the famous names had planted a vine.
Our new journal piece on a wine country with its own grapes and its own mind.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The white that made it home
item.description:
⟡ The white that made it home
For most of its life at the Cape it answered to another name. Steen, they called it, and they planted more of it than anything else without quite knowing what it was. The vines were not confidently identified as French Chenin Blanc until 1965, by which time the Cape had been growing it for three hundred years and had made it, in every sense that matters, its own.
More than half of all the Chenin Blanc in the world now grows in South Africa, more than in the Loire valley where it began. The Cape did not borrow this grape so much as adopt it and raise it as its own.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The one that exists nowhere else
item.description:
⟡ The one that exists nowhere else
In 1925 a professor at Stellenbosch named Abraham Perold crossed Pinot Noir with Cinsaut and planted four seeds in his garden. He very nearly lost them. The seedlings were rescued from a clearance by a young lecturer who happened to be passing, and from those vines came Pinotage, a grape that grows seriously almost nowhere on earth but here.
It has not always been easy to love, and that is part of the point. No one argues about a wine with no character. The Cape has a red that belongs to it completely, the way few countries can say of any grape, and the best of it now is very good indeed.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Trip That Earns Its Distance
item.description:
⟡ The Trip That Earns Its Distance
Long-haul destinations grew their share of UK holidays from seven per cent to ten in a single year. Australia, New Zealand, the Pacific Islands... places measured in days of flying, not hours. That figure comes from ABTA's Travel Trends 2026, and it is not a blip.
The far edge of the map is having a quiet revival. Our new Travel Intelligence piece looks at why the considered traveller is choosing one big trip over several small ones, and where that instinct leads.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Fewer trips, more room in each
item.description:
⟡ Fewer trips, more room in each
The 2026 Virtuoso Luxe Report describes travellers slowing down. Lingering longer once they arrive, choosing smaller camps and longer stays over a packed itinerary. Two-thirds of their advisors expect demand to rise next year, and more than half expect clients to spend more per trip.
People are not necessarily travelling more often. They are travelling further, and giving each trip more room. A twenty-four hour journey has never made sense for four nights. It makes a great deal of sense for two unhurried weeks.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The distance is part of the point
item.description:
⟡ The distance is part of the point
South Africa is a long way to go, and that is precisely why it rewards the traveller who stays. A fortnight there, the city and the winelands and the long slow afternoons, returns more than a string of short hops ever could.
The long trip is not an indulgence. It is the better use of the same time and the same money. Further, and then slower once you land... and you come home restored, rather than barely having had time to feel the place at all.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com
